The [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] measures {{cvt|445|-|532|mm|in}}, with a median of {{cvt|467|mm|in}} in males and {{cvt|512|mm|in}} in females.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name="Brown"/> In South Africa, 5 males averaged {{cvt|475.2|mm|in}} in wing chord length and 7 females averaged {{cvt|506.9|mm|in}}.<ref name=McPherson2/> While it, on average, is less heavy and has a smaller wingspan than the often sympatric [[martial eagle]], its average total length exceeds that of the martial eagle thanks to its much longer tail. The crowned eagle's tail is from {{cvt|300|to|410|mm|in}} long, with a median of {{cvt|315|mm|in}} in males and {{cvt|348|mm|in}} in females.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name="Brown"/> South African males averaged {{cvt|320.4|mm|in}} in tail length in a sample of 4 and females averaged {{cvt|352.4|mm|in}}.<ref name=McPherson2/> The [[Beak|bill]] is of a medium size relative to its body size, with one large museum specimen's bill measuring {{cvt|55|mm|in}} in length from the gape, {{cvt|45|mm|in}} in [[Culmen (bird)|culmen length]] and {{cvt|33|mm|in}} in bill depth.<ref name= Tabaranza>{{cite web |url= http://haringibon2011.wordpress.com/2008/03/09/haribon-haring-ibon-king-bird/ |title=Haribon – ''Ha ring mga Ibon'', King of Birds |first= Blas R. |last=Tabaranza Jr. |publisher=Haring Ibon's Flight… |access-date=24 June 2013|date=2008-03-09}}</ref> In South Africa, culmen length averaged {{cvt|50|mm|in}} in 4 males and {{cvt|54.9|mm|in}} in 7 females, with a range in both {{cvt|46.5|to|61.4|mm|in}}.<ref name=McPherson2/>

[[File:African Crowned Eagle 2.JPG|thumb|upright|A zoo-kept adult crowned eagle in [[Hungary]] illustrates its large talons. ]]
The [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] is of a modest length for a raptor of its size, at {{cvt|8.5|-|10.3|cm|in}}, and is clearly shorter than that of martial eagle.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> However, the feet and legs are visibly thicker and heavier than those of the martial eagle and the [[wikt:talon|talon]]s are apparently quite massive in both length and width. While few comprehensive measurements of the talon size of wild crowned eagles are known, one female museum specimen reportedly had a [[Claw|hallux-claw]] (or hind claw, which is the largest talon on accipitrids) of {{cvt|6.2|cm|in}},<ref name= Tabaranza/> while another female was measured at 5 years of age, the age of sexual maturity, {{cvt|5.74|cm|in}} in the hallux-claw and an adult male measured {{cvt|4.9|cm|in}}.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Prout-Jones, D. V.|author2=Kemp, A. G. |year=1997|title=Moult, plumage sequence and maintenance behaviour of a captive male and female crowned eagle, ''Stephanoaetus coronatus'' (Aves: Accipitridae)|journal=Annals of the Transvaal Museum|volume=36|issue=Part 19}}</ref> In South Africa, hallux-claw length averaged {{cvt|52.4|mm|in}} in 5 males and {{cvt|60|mm|in}} in 7 females with a range in both of {{cvt|48.6|to|61.4|mm|in}}.<ref name= McPherson/> These figures put their talon size as around the same size as the largest [[golden eagle]]s and half the size of a [[harpy eagle]].<ref name="Bortolotti">{{Cite journal |last=Bortolotti |first=G.R. |year=1984 |title=Age and sex size variation in Golden Eagles |url=https://www.usask.ca/biology/bortolotti/pubs/jfo-55-1-54-66.pdf |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |volume=55 |pages=54–66 |archive-date=31 October 2008 |access-date=4 January 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081031144116/https://www.usask.ca/biology/bortolotti/pubs/jfo-55-1-54-66.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title= Notes on the Harpy Eagle in British Guiana |journal = The Auk|volume = 81|issue = 3|pages = 257–273|last1=Fowler |first1=J.M. |last2=Cope |first2=J.B. |jstor = 4082683|year = 1964|doi = 10.2307/4082683}}</ref> Some captive crowned eagles have been credited with a hallux-claw length of up to {{cvt|10|cm|in}}, although, much like a single report of captive harpy eagles with a {{cvt|13|cm|in}} hallux-claw, no such outsized talons are known to have been confirmed.<ref name= Thomsett/><ref>{{cite web |url=http://animals.sandiegozoo.org/animals/harpy-eagle |title=Harpy Eagle |publisher=San Diego Zoo |access-date=24 June 2013}}</ref> In a small sampling of large, forest-dwelling raptors, the front-left talon of the crowned eagle, at {{cvt|4.74|cm|in}}, was around one cm less than that of a harpy eagle or the huge, recently extinct [[Haast's eagle]] (''Hieraaetus moorei'') and slightly smaller than those of the [[Philippine eagle]] (''Pithecophaga jefferyi'').<ref>Worthy, T.H. & Holdaway, R.N. 2002. The Lost World of the Moa: Prehistoric Life of New Zealand. [[Indiana University Press]], {{ISBN|0253340349}}.</ref> Considering a big female of these species can weigh up to twice as much as an average crowned eagle may illustrate the relative largeness of the crowned eagle's talons.<ref name = "CRC"/>

The adult crowned eagle is quite strikingly plumaged. Its crown is dark to rufous-tinged brown with a prominent, oft-raised black-tipped double crest, which can give the head a somewhat triangular appearance.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name="Brown"/> The upperparts of an adult are a blackish brown-grey color, with a variable tinge of blue. The throat is brown while the belly and breast are white overlaid densely with blackish bars and blotches, variably marked with cream or rich buff-rufous coloration. The [[Flight feather|wing primaries]] are white at the base, broadly tipped with black and crossed by two black bars. The tail is black with brownish-grey bands. The thighs and legs are barred and closely spotted with black and white. The underwing coverts of adults have a bold chestnut coloration, spotted lightly with black.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name="Brown"/> The adult crowned eagle has eyes that can range from yellow to almost white, a cere and feet of an ochre-yellow color and black talons.<ref name="Brown"/> In the wild, misidentification of an adult is improbable thanks to the species' bearing and voice.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> The strongly barred outer wings and tail are all diagnostic in flight.<ref name=subSahara/> Further simplifying identification, details such as the crest, the bird's upright perching posture and large size are unique to this animal.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> While they do differ somewhat in size, the sexes' [[Sexual dimorphism|dimorphism]] by size is relatively modest and eagles are unlikely to be sexed by this alone. However, the male may be distinguished by his more rapid wing beats (4 or 5 per second) from the more sluggish female (3 or 4 per second).<ref name="Brown"/>

{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Crowned Hawk-Eagle (Stephanoaetus coronatus) with prey ... (31850195390).jpg|desired_width=250|align=right|caption=Head close-up, in South Africa|crop_left_perc=52|crop_right_perc=23|crop_bottom_perc=57|crop_top_perc=20}}

As seen in about half of the "booted eagle" group, the juvenile crowned eagle has strikingly different looking plumage compared to the adults. Much variation occurs as the maturation process occurs. A great majority of juveniles have a white head and underside, which contrast with the thighs and legs, which are heavily spotted with black.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> The juvenile eagle's back is light brown or grayish-brown, with pale feather edgings that often give the back a scaled appearance, especially on the upper-wing coverts. There is often a pinkish red wash on the upper chest.<ref>Brown, L. H., & Pommery, D. E. (1984). ''The age structure of populations of wild birds in tropical Africa as demonstrated by plumage characters and marking techniques''. In Proceedings of the V Pan-African Ornithological Congress (pp. 97–119).</ref> Just-fledged chicks tend have dark patched faces, freckled bibs and slightly barred chests and spotted legs. Less common juvenile crowned eagle plumages, possible even when they are under a year of age and still under parental care, may include eagles so stripy that they which one could easily have aged as two- to three-year-olds.<ref name= Thomsett/> The tail of the juvenile is black with three pale bars and a narrow black tip. The juvenile eagle's cere is grey and the feet are dull yellow.<ref name="Brown"/> By 4 months post-fledgling, the inner thighs, previously poorly covered with downy type feathers, are covered with small feathers. While the pale 'morph' young just prior to leaving the nest usually have unmarked tarsus, they soon get spots on the front part of the tibio tarsal joint. The tibio tarsal pad is still bare and obvious up until it is a year old, whereupon it vanishes only to return to incubating females.<ref name= Thomsett/> Eye color is variable too with some having khaki light brown just prior to fledging and others with adult-like yellow ochre eyes.<ref name= Thomsett/> Up to 15 months after leaving the nest, the immature eagles more closely resemble the plumage they have at first independence than the adult's plumage.<ref name="Brown"/> The juvenile may be confused with the similarly colored juvenile martial eagle, especially in flight.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> It is distinguished from the martial species in having a much longer, more heavily barred tail, much shorter wings and spotted thighs.<ref name=subSahara/>

== Habitat ==
The crowned eagle inhabits mainly dense [[woodland]]s, including those deep within [[Tropical rainforest|rainforest]], but will sometimes also be found in relict patches, wooded escarpments, [[riparian]] strips of ''[[Acacia]]'', heavily wooded hillsides, and rocky outcrops throughout its range. The crowned eagle may be found from an altitude of sea-level to at least {{cvt|3000|m|ft|-1}}. Owing to lack of current suitable habitat, the eagle's range is often somewhat discontinuous.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /> [[File:Fluss Dja Somalomo.JPG|thumb|left|Humid [[Congolian forests]] along the [[Dja River]], typical habitat near the center of the crowned eagle's range]]In the [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]], the crowned eagle has been confirmed to survive at relatively high densities in protected areas that maintain dense, old-growth rainforests.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.friendsofbonobos.org/Bonobo%20and%20their%20Habitat%20-%206-11%20years%20-%20lessons%20plan.pdf |title=Bonobos and their Habitat |first=Alice |last=Hare |publisher=Friends of Bonobos |access-date=24 June 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121024204431/http://www.friendsofbonobos.org/Bonobo%20and%20their%20Habitat%20-%206-11%20years%20-%20lessons%20plan.pdf |archive-date=24 October 2012}}</ref> In [[Kenya]], 84% of the crowned eagles range is within rainforest with an annual rainfall amount of more than {{cvt|150|cm|in}}.<ref>{{cite book|author=Lewis, A.|author2=Pomeroy, D.|title=A Bird Atlas of Kenya|publisher=CRC Press|year=1989 |isbn=90-6191-716-6}}</ref> Around stretches of [[East Africa]] where protected areas mostly consist of fairly open habitat, crowned eagles usually live in wooded areas of rocky hills and narrow riverine strips, only rarely ranging into savanna surrounding the hills.<ref name="Brown" /> [[Southern Africa]] has been subject to the most comprehensive study of crowned eagle habitat, largely since many areas there would seem inhospitable to a large raptor often associated with old-growth forest.<ref name="Oatley">Oatley, T.B., Oschadleus, H.D., Navarro, R.A. and Underhill, L.G. 1998. ''Review of ring recoveries of birds of prey in southern Africa: 1948–1998''. Johannesburg: Endangered Wildlife Trust.</ref> In southern Africa, its distribution south of the [[Limpopo River]] coincides largely with [[Montane ecology|montane forest]], although it is not restricted to that habitat and may range secondarily into [[plantation]]s, usually of [[eucalyptus]].<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Oatley" /> In South Africa, it occurs in both lowland and montane [[evergreen forest]], dense woodland, and forested ravines and gorges in open [[savanna]]s and [[Veld|thornveld]].<ref name="Boshoff">Boshoff, A.F. 1997. Crowned Eagle. Pp. 194–195 in J.A. Harrison et al. (eds.), ''The atlas of South African birds''. Volume 1: Non-passerines. BirdLife South Africa and Avian Demography Unit, Johannesburg, South Africa.</ref> In [[Zimbabwe]], the crowned eagle can be found in quite open woods with ''[[Adansonia]]'' trees and may occasionally forage in savanna and secondary growth.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /> In [[Malawi]], highland birds forage in lower [[Miombo|miombo woodland]], and lower altitudes, breeding occurs in deciduous forest, more locally in dense miombo, tall riparian woodlands, and in remnants close to cultivation.<ref name="Dowsett" /> Crowned eagles in [[Zambezi]], occurs in evergreen forest in the eastern highlands, in rugged, hilly terrain over the central plateau, in hills and escarpments in the southeastern portions of the central watershed, and in riparian habitat along the larger rivers.<ref name="Irwin">{{cite book|author=Irwin, M. P. S. |year=1981|title=The birds of Zimbabwe|publisher=Quest Publishing|location=Salisbury, Zimbabwe}}</ref>

==Behavior==
[[File:Crownedeagle.jpg|thumb|right|A crowned eagle flying over its territory in Budongo, western Uganda]]
Typical of most raptors that breed in Africa, the crowned eagle is non-migratory and is largely sedentary.<ref name= Oatley/> This species usually inhabits a fixed territory throughout the year during its adult life. There is evidence that the birds move about to some degree when circumstances require it, for example when they need to change mates in isolated breeding areas.<ref name="Brown"/> The greatest movement of any notable distance is usually associated with juvenile birds, which wander relatively widely before maturation.<ref name= Oatley/><ref>Bildstein, K.L. 2006. ''Migrating raptors of the world: their ecology and conservation''. [[Cornell University Press]], Ithaca, NY.</ref> Forty-four birds of various ages that were ringed in southern Africa and recovered were all found near the ringing localities.<ref name= Oatley/> Small movements by crowned eagles has resulted in them being sighted in improbable habitats, such as on an open savanna hill in [[Kenya]], an open river bed in [[Lewa Wildlife Conservancy]] and even a golf course in [[Nairobi]].<ref name= Thomsett/>

The crowned eagle is highly vocal and has a noisy, undulating display flight. In [[Equatorial Africa]], they often call year-around, while elsewhere they may vocalize mainly in the context of breeding and nesting activities.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> The call is a series of loud whistles that rise and fall in pitch.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> The male performs an elaborate rise-and-fall display over the forest canopy both during the breeding season and outside it as a territorial proposition.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Usually, territorial displays, which outnumber breeding displays, occur around the periphery of the bird's home range while breeding displays are likely to be over or at least near the nest.<ref name="Brown"/> Displays consists of a series of steep dives and ascents, with a few wing-flaps at the top of each climb and descending circles and figures of eight.<ref name="Brown"/> During descents, eagles can drop as much as {{cvt|60|m|ft}} at a time before circling back up.<ref name= Leslie/> During this display, the male is noisy, uttering a shrill ''kewee-kewee-kewee'' while throwing his head back, often calling for a spell of approximately 30 seconds.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> The displaying male may reach heights exceeding {{cvt|900|m|ft|-1}}, sometimes even near cloud level at over {{cvt|2000|m|ft|-1}} above the ground.<ref name="Brown"/> The adult female may also perform independent display flights, uttering a lower ''kooee-kooee-kooee''. The female seems to display less often and tends to have a mellower voice.<ref name="Brown"/> Pairs also perform visually striking mutual displays, sometimes arising from the first type or when the pairs come together after a brief absence.<ref name="Brown"/> Spectacular tandems, interlocking talons and falling some distance from the sky are typical of mutual displays.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Despite the spectacular and conspicuous nature of their display flights, the crowned eagle is sometimes considered a "clumsy" flier out in the open, lacking the grace and speed attributed to other eagles in a high-flying context, such as large ''Aquila'' species.<ref name= Leslie/> While awaiting food at the nest, both the female and the young call out a penetrating, high ''kwee-kwee-kwee'', that can border on incessant.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/>

In disposition, the crowned eagle is considered nervous, constantly alert and on edge.<ref name= Thomsett/> They are quite different from the oft-stately open country eagles such as the ''[[Aquila (genus)|Aquila]]'' species. In their training and management, crowned eagles are perhaps more reminiscent of a [[Eurasian goshawk|Goshawk]] (''Accipiter gentilis'') than ''Aquila'' eagles.<ref name= Thomsett/> Some linkage has been made between forest-dwelling habits and having a curious and edgy disposition amongst various raptorial birds.<ref name="Brown"/><ref name="Thomsett"/> Ironically considering their otherwise hyperactive behavior, the main hunting techniques of crowned eagle require long periods of inactivity, spent sitting on a perch.<ref name= Leslie/> Mature crowned eagles are reportedly nearly fearless towards humans and, unless shy from prior interactions, unusually prone to treat humans aggressively.<ref name= Leslie/> Crowned eagles can nest around developed areas, including in the vicinity of quite suburban, developed areas (such as in view of apartment or office buildings), so long as prey is abundant and accessible and the habitat provides enough mature vegetation to facilitate nest building and hunting activities.<ref name= Leslie/> Some biologists consider this species highly intelligent, cautious, independent and inquisitive when compared to other accipitrids.<ref name= Thomsett/> In [[falconry]], crowned eagles cannot be induced to direct their hunting instinct towards large prey by increasing their hunger, as is done with ''Aquila'' eagles, for example.<ref name= Thomsett/> In the context of human interaction, wild, adult crowned eagles are quite the opposite of the [[martial eagle]], which are typically very wary and tends to shun any variety of human activity.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> However, young crowned eagles in their post-fledging stage differ greatly in behavior from independent or adult eagles. Amongst post-fledging eagles in a semi-captive state, it has been noted that they border on helpless in terms of feeding and defending themselves compared to other accipitrids and are even described as "cowardly", unwilling to even simulate attacking prey until many months after fledging. This implies a learning element occurs in wild crowned eagles during their exceptionally long post-fledging period.<ref name= Thomsett/> Crowned eagles are reportedly variable in temperament as individuals to a degree greater than that found in most other raptors.<ref name= Thomsett/>

===Breeding===
Crowned eagles have one of the most prolonged breeding cycles of any bird. It is common for raptors that live around the tropics to have a relatively elongated breeding period.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Crowned eagle pairs breed once every two years; a single breeding cycle is approximately 500 days in duration.<ref name="Brown"/> Most other eagle species complete a breeding cycle in under six months, or in about 35% of the time it takes the crowned eagle.<ref name= Leslie/> While the incubation and nestling stages are about average for a tropical eagle (for example the [[black-breasted snake-eagle]] (''Circaetus pectoralis''), about half the weight of this species, has an incubation/nestling cycle of a similar length), it is the extraordinary post-fledging period of 9–11 months that makes the crowned eagles' breeding cycle so long.<ref name= Leslie/> In [[harpy eagle|harpy]] and [[Philippine eagle]], although these are less extensively studied, it may take a similar or even longer amount of time for the young to attain full independence.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Rettig |first=Neil |date=1978-10-01 |title=Breeding behavior of the Harpy Eagle (Harpia harpyja) |url=https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/95/4/629/5208729 |journal=The Auk |volume=95 |issue=4 |pages=629–643 |doi=10.1093/auk/95.4.629 |doi-broken-date=11 July 2025 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>Delacour, J., E. Mayr. 1946. ''Birds of the Philippines''. New York: The MacMillan Company.</ref> A case of crowned eagle pairs that reportedly bred every year in South Africa are unsubstantiated but may be due to an apparently high population loss rate among juvenile eagles near areas that are heavily populated by humans.<ref name= Batchelor/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brown |first1=L. H. |last2=Gargett |first2=V. |last3=Steyn |first3=P. |date=December 1977 |title=Breeding success in some African eagles related to theories about sibling aggression and its effects |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00306525.1977.9634083 |journal=Ostrich |language=en |volume=48 |issue=3–4 |pages=65–71 |doi=10.1080/00306525.1977.9634083 |bibcode=1977Ostri..48...65B |issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Breeding can occur almost year-around throughout the range, though egg laying seems to peak roughly around the end of the African [[wet season]] or the early [[dry season]], from July to November.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Territories or home ranges are maintained vigorously. In [[Zimbabwe]], individual home ranges can vary from {{cvt|140|to|200|km2|sqmi|-1}} in size.<ref name= Irwin/> Near the city of [[Nelspruit]] in South Africa, home ranges averaged {{cvt|30|km2|sqmi|-1}} in size.<ref name= Batchelor/> In southern Africa, the mean distance between active nest sites can range from {{cvt|2|to|19.5|km|mi|-1}}.<ref name= Boshoff/>

After engaging in the breeding display described above, the pair collaborate in building a massive nest in a fork of a large forest tree, typically from {{cvt|12|to|45|m|ft|-1}} above the ground. While the female fetches more nesting material, the male tends to be more active in nest construction.<ref name="Brown"/> In [[East Africa]], many nests appear to be close to a forest river.<ref name="Brown"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Maisels |first1=Fiona G. |last2=Gautier |first2=Jean-Pierre |last3=Cruickshank |first3=Alick |last4=Bosefe |first4=Jean-Pierre |date=1993-02-08 |title=Attacks by Crowned Hawk Eagles (''Stephanoaetus coronatus'') on Monkeys in Zaire |url=https://brill.com/view/journals/ijfp/61/3/article-p157_6.xml |journal=Folia Primatologica |volume=61 |issue=3 |pages=157–159 |doi=10.1159/000156743 |issn=0015-5713|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Generally, crowned eagles seem to be attracted to the taller trees in the forest.<ref name= Batchelor/> On the [[Nyika Plateau]] in [[Malawi]], the favorite nesting trees are the large emergent ''[[Aningeria adolfi-friederici]]'' and ''[[Gambeya gorungosana]]'', and a pair in the Lower Shire used a ''[[Sterculia appendiculata]]'' tree.<ref name= Dowsett/> In [[Zimbabwe]], ''[[Newtonia buchananii]]'' are reportedly one of the most used tree species for nesting.<ref name= Irwin/> Exceptional crowned eagle nests have been observed on sheer cliff faces.<ref name= Steyn/> In southern Africa, the species nest in drier and denuded terrain than expected such as ''[[Adansonia]]'' stands on semi-arid hillsides. Despite the relative sparseness of this habitat, these sites have a varied and convoluted terrain, with nooks and crannies, valleys, overhangs and hideaways that allow a crowned eagle to exercise its particular hunting skills. In Kenya, similar fractured landscapes can also be utilized by crowned eagles, such as the black gigantic volcanic rubble fields of [[Tsavo West National Park]], the lower [[Chyulu Hills]], [[Kibwezi]] and [[Soysambu Conservancy]]. These are jungles of boulders covered with low growth interspersed (in the past) with high trees.<ref name= Thomsett/> A nest built from scratch may take up to 5 months to construct, however existing nests are often repaired and re-used during successive breeding seasons, a process that can take as much as 3 months.<ref name="Brown"/> It is typical for an eagle pair to use a nest for more than five years and, unlike several other booted eagles, crowned eagle pairs rarely build more than one nest for alternative use.<ref name= Leslie/> Most large eagles build a very large nest and the crowned eagle is no exception, as it builds one of the largest nests of any eagle. In the first year they build a nest, it may measure {{cvt|1.5|m|ft}} across and {{cvt|50|cm|in}} deep. However, a larger nest, usually after several years of usage, may measure up to {{cvt|2.5|m|ft}} across and up to {{cvt|3|m|ft}} deep. The nest consists of both dead and greener branches and has a light coverage of leaves and animal matter.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Copulation takes place in the nest, several times a day. Reportedly copulation can occur up to a year before laying, although these may be exceptional cases of mating for non-fertilization purposes (which, in other eagles, has been thought to be related mainly to strengthening pair bonds).<ref name="Brown"/> A pre-copulation display typically occurs, wherein the male runs repeatedly around the crouching female with wings upraised, which displays the chestnut of the under-wing coverts and beautiful barring.<ref name="Brown"/>

In South Africa the crowned eagle lays its eggs from September to October; in [[Zimbabwe]], it lays from May to October; mainly nearer to October around the [[Congo River]]; anywhere from June to November in [[Kenya]], with a peak in August through October; in Uganda from December to July; and in [[West Africa]], laying peaks in October.<ref name="Brown"/> The clutch of the crowned eagle either contains 1 or 2 eggs. Often in [[East Africa]], just one egg is laid.<ref name="Brown"/> Eggs are usually just white, though may sometimes be overlaid with sparse red-brown markings. The eggs are moderate in size, averaging {{cvt|68.2|x|53.6|mm|in}}, with ranges of {{cvt|60.9|-|75.5|mm|in}} in length and {{cvt|50.8|-|57.9|mm|in}} in width.<ref name="Brown"/> When a natural disaster befalls a nest, a replacement may be made in 2 months time.<ref name="Brown"/> Incubation lasts for approximately 49 days. 80–90% of egg incubation is done by the female during the day.<ref name="Brown"/> Food is mainly brought to the nest by the male in the early stages of breeding, though sometimes both sexes may deliver food. The male brings food to the incubating female every 3 to 5 days. When they initially hatch, the young tend to be quite quiet. If two eggs are laid, the younger one dies by starvation after being outcompeted for food by the older one or even directly killed by its older sibling.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> No nest of wild crowned eagles has been known to successfully produce more than one fledgling, though in captivity two have been known to survive with human assistance (supplementally feeding the younger chick or taking it out of the nest).<ref name= Thomsett/><ref name= Batchelor/> In cases where the older nestling dies, the younger one may be fed more regularly and survive.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/>

After hatching, the male kill rate rises to one kill every 1.5 days. Pair behavior while raising chicks is very variable, some males are very attentive to their young, while others leave virtually all brooding to the female. After 40 days of age, the young is capable of feeding itself, though is often still fed. The first feathers through the white down emerge when the crowned eagle chick is 40 days old, with the feathers ultimately covering the down in 76 days. After 76 days, the main feather development is in the tail or the wings. Wing flapping begins at 45 to 50 days, increasing after around 75 days.<ref name="Brown"/> The young fledge at 90 to 115 days, with an average 110.6 days and any period of time less than 100 days is considered unusually soon. On average, male chicks tend to be more active wing-flappers and usually will first fly around 10 days earlier than female chicks.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> After fledging, females are attentive 95% of the day and brood 50–75% of the day, the amount decreasing slightly with each day. The female does much of the prey capture and a majority of the nest defense after the young fledge. After fledging, the young remain in the neighborhood of the parent's nest and are fed every 3 to 5 days by either parent for their first 270–350 days of life.<ref name="Brown"/> The rate of food-delivery varies from several times a day to every 3 days on average during the post-fledging period. The fledged juvenile will solicit adults (apparently even unrelated adults) for food but does not actually take the prey unless this occurs around the nest site.<ref name="Brown"/> The first recorded kill for a young crowned occurred 61 days after fledging, although this is considered exceptionally early by the standards of this species.<ref name="Brown"/> Flights increase incrementally through the post-fledging period, although the young do not engage in rising flights until they are fully independent. Independence appears to be triggered by the increased indifference of parents to bringing food. Due to the loud vocal interplay between the parents and the fledging eagle, the adults seem to take it as a sign that their offspring has sought independence if they return to the nesting area and hear no begging auditory response.<ref name= Leslie/> The young eagle usually remains in the care of its parents for a total of up to 11 months after it fledges, longer than is known in almost any other raptor. The advantage of this prolonged stretch to independence is that it may make for a stronger young eagle when compared to other accipitrids which have almost no post-fledging dependency period.<ref name= Leslie/> In 34 possible cases, 18 resulted in eggs being laid. Fledging success is approximately 83% and almost all young that leave the nest also reach independence.<ref name="Brown"/>  
It is estimated that most crowned eagles will reach breeding maturity at around five years old, as is typical for other large eagle species.<ref name= Leslie/>

==Dietary biology==
The crowned eagle is often described as the most powerful raptor in Africa, even more so than the two slightly heavier species endemic to Africa, the [[martial eagle]] and the [[Verreaux's eagle]] (''Aquila verreauxii'').<ref name="Brown"/><ref name= Steyn>Steyn, P. 1982. Birds of prey of southern Africa: their identification and life histories. David Phillip, Cape Town, South Africa.</ref> One listing included the crowned eagle as the only bird in a ranking of the 10 strongest living land creatures (pound for pound).<ref name= Telegraph>{{cite news |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/earth/earthpicturegalleries/7521275/The-strongest-living-land-creatures-on-Earth-measured-by-their-power-to-weight-ratio.html?image=4 |title=The strong living land creatures on Earth, measured by their power to weight ratio |work=The Daily Telegraph|location=London |access-date=24 June 2013|date=2010-03-25 }}</ref> Elsewhere, the [[harpy eagle]] is listed as the overall most powerful living eagle and bird of prey.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref>Schulenberg, T.S. (2009) ''Harpy eagle (Harpia harpyja)''. In: Schulenberg, T.S. (Ed.) Neotropical Birds Online. Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca.</ref> Since there are no known actual tests in any African raptor of the pressure exerted via their grip, as has been done with some other large eagles, their power has been estimated from the size of the feet and talons and from the prey they typically select.<ref name="Brown"/>

The crowned eagle's staple diet is mostly mammalian. One estimate of the typical prey range posited that the weight range is from {{cvt|1|to|5|kg|lb}}, which is, based on the species ecology in [[Kenya]]n hillside woodland. This prey weight range is roughly the same weight range that's typically attributed to martial or Verreaux's eagles.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Brown |first1=L. H. | year = 1963 | title = Observations on East African birds of prey | journal = African Journal of Ecology | volume = 1 | issue = 1| pages = 5–17 | doi=10.1111/j.1365-2028.1963.tb00174.x|bibcode=1963AfJEc...1....5B }}</ref> Perhaps unsurprisingly, that is the weight range of the [[rock hyrax]], which all three large eagles are known to hunt regularly in [[East Africa]].<ref>{{cite web |url= http://www.afrotheria.net/Hyracoidea.html |title=Hyraxes |publisher= IUCN SSC Afrotheria Specialist Group |access-date=24 June 2013}}</ref> In a large collection of bones in the [[South African Museum]], it was found that 51.2% of the bones collected from [[Nature's Valley]] in South Africa were from smallish species which weigh under {{cvt|2|kg|lb}} in adulthood, 26.3% from "medium-sized" species with an adult weight of {{cvt|2|to|20|kg|lb}} and 22.5% were from larger species with an adult weight of over {{cvt|20|kg|lb}}.<ref name= Tarboton>{{cite journal|author=Tarboton, W.R.|author2=Allan, D.G. |year=1984|title=The Status and conservation of Birds of Prey in the Transvaal|location=Pretoria|journal=Transvaal Museum Monograph|volume=3}}</ref> However, about 91% of 87 bones that were from the relatively large antelope species, amongst those that could be accurately sized, were from juvenile specimens.<ref name= Tarboton/> On the other hand, in the rainforest community of [[Taï National Park]] in the [[Ivory Coast]], the estimated average weight of prey for crowned eagles was clearly higher at {{cvt|5.67|kg|lb}}.<ref name= Shultz>{{cite journal | last1=Shultz |first1=S. |last2=Noë |first2=R. |last3=McGraw |first3=W. S. |last4=Dunbar |first4=R. I. M. | year = 2004 | title = A community-level evaluation of the impact of prey behavioural and ecological characteristics on predator diet composition | journal = Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences | volume = 271 | issue = 1540| pages = 725–732 | doi=10.1098/rspb.2003.2626|pmid=15209106 | pmc = 1691645 |bibcode=2004PBioS.271..725S }}</ref> Of all other living eagles, only the female harpy eagle has been credited with an average prey weight range that is comparably high and, at the species level, the crowned eagle's prey size from the latter study is the largest of any known for all extant accipitrids.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> The crowned eagle is perhaps the only living accipitrid to routinely attack prey weighing in excess of {{cvt|9|kg|lb}}.<ref name= Leslie/> According to some authorities typical prey sizes for crowned eagle may be considered to range to at least {{cvt|20|kg|lb}} in body mass.<ref name="McGraw, W. S. 2006"/><ref name="Shultz2">{{Cite journal |last=Shultz |first=Susanne |date=February 2002 |title=Population density, breeding chronology and diet of Crowned Eagles ''Stephanoaetus coronatus'' in Taï National Park, Ivory Coast |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1046/j.0019-1019.2001.00005.x |journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=144 |issue=1 |pages=135–138 |doi=10.1046/j.0019-1019.2001.00005.x |bibcode=2002Ibis..144..135S |issn=0019-1019|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="McPherson">{{Cite journal |last1=McPherson |first1=Shane C. |last2=Brown |first2=Mark |last3=Downs |first3=Colleen T. |date=March 2016 |title=Diet of the crowned eagle (''Stephanoaetus coronatus'') in an urban landscape: potential for human-wildlife conflict? |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s11252-015-0500-6 |journal=Urban Ecosystems |language=en |volume=19 |issue=1 |pages=383–396 |doi=10.1007/s11252-015-0500-6 |bibcode=2016UrbEc..19..383M |issn=1083-8155|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Reportedly, the crowned eagle can lift more than its own body weight in flight, though verified accounts of this are sparse.<ref name= Ginn>Ginn, P.J., McIlleorn, W.G. & le S Milstein, P. 1989. ''The Complete Book of Southern African Birds''. Johnnesburg: Strake Winchester.</ref>

In a deep forest, an adult eagle may cover a hunting range of up to {{cvt|6.5|to|16|km2|sqmi|-1}}, with the home ranges being smaller for those that inhabit rocky hills and cliffs abounding in [[hyrax]]es.<ref name="Brown"/> Eagles start hunting soon after dawn and mainly kill early in the morning and in the evening prior to sundown.<ref name="Brown"/> Being a forest-dwelling species, the crowned eagle has no need to travel great distances to hunt, nor employ a great deal of active hunting flight (such as soaring seen in [[savannah]]-dwelling species). Rather, it tends to hunt passively.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Crowned eagles may locate a suitable hunting spot by listening (such as via the call of the noisy [[vervet monkey]]) or watching for prey activity, though may also use habitual hunting perches where they've previously had hunting successes.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name="Brown"/> Although this behavior is unconfirmed, some crowned eagles have been reported to let out a soft whistle, unlike their other vocalizations which, for some reason, is attractive to monkeys and will then attack the first monkey to enter their line of sight.<ref name="Brown"/> These eagles often still-hunt, wherein they drop or stoop onto prey from a branch perch. Following the sighting of suitable prey, the eagle quickly and stealthily maneuvers itself through the forest towards its prey, a certain element of surprise inherent in its final approach.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> A majority of the crowned eagle's kills are made on the [[forest floor]]. Arboreal prey may be forced to the ground during an attack.<ref name= Leslie/> The sharp, powerful talons may produce sufficient force to kill the prey on impact; if not, death from trauma or [[asphyxiation]] soon follows.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Several prey items have been killed by ramming the talons into the skull and penetrating the brain.<ref>Wieczkowski, J., McGraw, W. S., & Butynski, T. M. (2012). ''Inferred African Crowned Eagle (''Stephanoaetus coronatus'') Predation on a Tana River Mangabey (''Cercocebus galeritus'')''. African Primates, 7(2), Pp 218.</ref> Having killed on the ground, it has the ability to fly almost vertically upwards to a branch while carrying its prey before feeding, though it will tear up prey into manageable pieces on the ground when it is exceedingly heavy.<ref name="Brown"/> While they both attack somewhat similar prey in often similar habitat, the considerable difference in body weight and wing-loading between crowned and harpy eagles have been attributed to load-carrying while hunting, as harpies tend to capture and carry off most prey in active flight rather than an attack on the ground and dismember if necessary.<ref name="Brown"/>

On rare occasions, crowned eagles may also hunt on the wing, flying slightly over the canopy and causing a cacophony amongst monkey groups until they detect and capture their prey, often a monkey or tree hyrax.<ref name="Brown"/> Crowned eagles are believed to take uneaten portions of prey up into the trees to cache around the nest or habitual perches so that the pieces can be consumed over the course of the next several days.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> If the prey is too heavy for taking flight with, even after dismemberment, for example, a [[Cape bushbuck|bushbuck]], crowned eagles have been known to cache food at the thickly vegetated base of a tree and only carry limbs to the nest.<ref name= Leslie/> Pairs may collaborate in capturing prey, with one bird flushing the prey so the other can glide in unseen and ambush it.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> Female eagles may target male monkeys more often than males, which are more likely to hunt female or young monkeys.<ref name= Thomsett/> In one case, a female crowned eagle stalked a [[Cape bushbuck|bushbuck]] calf over the course of two days but was repeatedly foiled when it went in for the attack, either by the mother bushbuck or an associating troop of [[yellow baboon]]s (''Papio cyanocephalus''). However, the crowned eagle one day assaulted the bushbuck calf quickly, leaving it with a gaping wound on its flank, and flew off to observe from a distance. Within a few more days, the bleeding, wounded calf was unable to keep pace with its mother and was tracked and killed by the assaulting eagle.<ref name= Thomsett/> Another assault, this one on an adult [[vervet monkey]] (''Chlorocebus pygerythrus'') apparently had a similar outcome as the bushbuck attack.<ref name= Thomsett/> This type of strike-and-wait hunting technique may be used by diverse predators, from [[Komodo dragon]]s (''Varanus komodoensis'') to [[great white shark]]s (''Carcharodon carcharias''), which tend to track their victims by scent after biting them rather than sight and sound, but is virtually unprecedented in birds.<ref name= Thomsett/> Crowned eagles have been recorded to consume carrion but this behavior has only rarely been observed.<ref name="Brown"/>

===Primate===
[[File:FranceNormandieChamprepusCercopithecusDiana.jpg|thumb|left|A [[Diana monkey]] (''Cercopithecus diana''), a favored monkey in the crowned eagle's diet]]
The crowned eagle occupies a unique niche, as it is the only bird in which [[primate]]s are the most commonly taken prey at the species level.<ref name="Mitani">{{cite journal |last1=Mitani |first1=J.C. |last2=Sanders |first2=W.J. |last3=Lwanga |first3=J.S. |last4=Windfelder |first4=T.L. |name-list-style=amp |year=2001 |title=''Predatory behavior of crowned hawk-eagles (''Stephanoaetus coronatus'') in Kibale National Park, Uganda'' |url=https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/2027.42/42089/1/265-49-2-3-187_10490187.pdf |journal=Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology |volume=49 |issue=2–3 |pages=187–195 |bibcode=2001BEcoS..49..187M |doi=10.1007/s002650000283 |s2cid=19062636 |hdl-access=free |hdl=2027.42/42089}}</ref> While at least a dozen other accipitrids opportunistically hunt small or young [[monkey]]s, only the [[harpy eagle]] and, possibly, [[crested eagle]] of the [[neotropic]]s have diets where primates may locally outnumber other prey. However, [[New World monkeys]] are generally smaller and less formidable than [[Old World monkey]]s. Also, both other eagles may prefer different prey where available: harpy eagles primarily take [[sloth]]s, which can be as heavy as a [[colobus]] or [[mangabey]] but are much slower and less able to defend themselves, while crested eagles take a variety of tropical birds and arboreal mammals. Other large eagles have been confirmed to hunt adult [[Old World monkeys]], including [[martial eagle]]s,<ref name="Baldellou">{{Cite journal |last1=Baldellou |first1=Maribel |last2=Peter Henzi |first2=S. |date=March 1992 |title=Vigilance, predator detection and the presence of supernumerary males in vervet monkey troops |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0003347205801046 |journal=Animal Behaviour |language=en |volume=43 |issue=3 |pages=451–461 |doi=10.1016/S0003-3472(05)80104-6|bibcode=1992AnBeh..43..451B |url-access=subscription }}</ref> [[Verreaux's eagle]]s,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Chiweshe |first1=N. | year = 2007 | title = Black Eagles and hyraxes—the two flagship species in the conservation of wildlife in the Matobo Hills, Zimbabwe | journal = [[Ostrich (journal)|Ostrich: Journal of African Ornithology]] | volume = 78 | issue = 2| pages = 381–386 | doi=10.2989/ostrich.2007.78.2.42.122|bibcode=2007Ostri..78..381C |s2cid=84596174 }}</ref> [[mountain hawk-eagle]]s<ref>{{cite journal | last = Iida |first=T | year = 1999 | title = Predation of Japanese macaque Macaca fuscata by mountain hawk eagle Spizaetus nipalensis | journal = Japanese Journal of Ornithology | volume = 47 | issue = 3| pages = 125–127 | doi=10.3838/jjo.47.125| doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Fam |first1=S. D. |last2=Nijman |first2=V. |date=April 2011 |title=Spizaetus hawk-eagles as predators of arboreal colobines |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s10329-011-0240-z |journal=Primates |language=en |volume=52 |issue=2 |pages=105–110 |doi=10.1007/s10329-011-0240-z |issn=0032-8332 |pmid= 21340695|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and [[Philippine eagle]]s, whose generic scientific name and old common was even the monkey-eating eagle,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Salvador |first1=D. J. |last2=Ibanez |first2=J. C. | year = 2006 | title = Ecology and conservation of Philippine Eagles | journal = Ornithological Science | volume = 5 | issue = 2| pages = 171–176 | doi=10.2326/osj.5.171}}</ref> but all are believed to rely on non-primate prey for the majority of their diet.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/>

The favored group in the crowned eagle's diet is certainly the genus ''[[Cercopithecus]]''. In [[Kibale National Park]], [[Uganda]], the [[red-tailed monkey]] (''Cercopithecus ascanius'') was the most represented prey species, making up 40% of the remains. Larger monkeys, such as [[western red colobus]] (''Piliocolobus badius''), [[mantled guereza]] (''Colobus guereza'') and [[grey-cheeked mangabey]] (''Lophocebus albigena'') were secondary in the prior study and, altogether, primates made up 82.2% of the remains from two nests there.<ref name= Struhsaker>{{cite journal | last1 = Struhsaker |first1=T. T. |last2=Leakey |first2=M. | year = 1990 | title = Prey selectivity by crowned hawk-eagles on monkeys in the Kibale Forest, Uganda | journal = [[Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology]] | volume = 26 | issue = 6| pages = 435–443 | doi=10.1007/bf00170902|bibcode=1990BEcoS..26..435S |s2cid=21024373 }}</ref> A total of 88% of the remains found around crowned eagle nests in the [[Ituri Rainforest]] in the [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]] were from primates: [[blue monkey]] (''Cercopithecus mitis''), [[red-tailed monkey]], [[Wolf's mona monkey]] (''C. wolfi''), [[western red colobus]] and a mixture of [[mangabey]]s and [[black-and-white colobus]].<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Hart |first1=J. |last2=Katembo |first2=M. |last3=Punga |first3=K. | name-list-style=amp | year = 1996 | title = Diet, prey selection and ecological relations of leopard and golden cat in the Ituri Forest, Zaire | journal = [[African Journal of Ecology]] | volume = 34 | issue = 4| pages = 364–379 | doi=10.1111/j.1365-2028.1996.tb00632.x|bibcode=1996AfJEc..34..364H }}</ref> In 16 nests in the [[Ivory Coast]]'s [[Taï National Park|Taï Forest]], more than 60% of remains around the eyries were monkeys and more than 45% of remains were from ''Cercopithecus'' monkeys. About half of the ''Cercopithecus'' remains were from [[Diana monkey]] (''C. diana''; 44 items from 28 adults and 16 immatures), with the other half from [[Campbell's mona monkey]] (''C. campbelli'') and [[lesser spot-nosed monkey]] (''C. petaurista''), which were indistinguishable as remains. Other represented monkeys in the Taï Forest included the Western red colobus, the [[olive colobus]] (''Procolobus verus'') and the [[king colobus]] (''Colobus polykomos'').<ref name="McGraw, W. S. 2006"/> The diet is, by necessity, more diverse in [[Kiwengwa/Pongwe Forest Reserve]] in the Matumbi Hills of [[Tanzania]] but the [[blue monkey]] was still the most represented prey species, making up 20% of the remains. In one nest in the prior study, the Blue monkey made up more than 90% of the remains.<ref name= Msuya>Msuya, C. A. (1993). "FEEDING HABITS OF CROWNED EAGLES Stephanoaetus coronatus IN KIWENGOMA FOREST RESERVE, MATUMBI HILLS, TANZANIA". In ''Annales Musee Royal de l'Afrique Centrale''. Sciences Zoologiques (Vol. 268).</ref> Where they overlap in range, the [[vervet monkey]] can be quite prominent as prey for crowned eagles, as their relatively small size, diurnal and terrestrial habits may make them more vulnerable.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Willems |first1=E. P. |name-list-style=amp |last2=Hill |first2=R. A. |year=2009 |title=Predator-specific landscapes of fear and resource distribution: effects on spatial range use |url=https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/32114/2/Ecology_Revised_MS_08-0765.pdf |journal=Ecology |volume=90 |issue=2 |pages=546–555 |doi=10.1890/08-0765.1 |pmid=19323238 |bibcode=2009Ecol...90..546W |access-date=27 September 2020 |archive-date=10 July 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210710012329/https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/32114/2/Ecology_Revised_MS_08-0765.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hall |first1=K. R. L. |last2=Gartlan |first2=J. S. |date=May 1965 |title=ECOLOGY AND BEAVIOUR OF THE VERVET MONKEY, ''CERCOPITHECUS AETHIOPS'' , LOLUI ISLAND, LAKE VICTORIA |url=https://zslpublications.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1469-7998.1965.tb01999.x |journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London |language=en |volume=145 |issue=1 |pages=37–56 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.1965.tb01999.x |issn=0370-2774|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Struhsaker and Leakey found that among both small and large species, adult male monkeys were often better represented in the diet than adult females.<ref name= Struhsaker/> This may be a result of the behavior of male primates. In some species, only adult males often travel separately from social groups, only adult males take aggressive action against eagles, and only adult males have loud calls that may attract the attention of eagles.<ref name= Struhsaker/><ref name= Arlet/> The variation in behavior of primates is illustrated so that in [[red colobus|red colobus species]], in which adult males patrol in mixed groups, the males are rarely attacked; meanwhile in the [[black-and-white colobus]], in which adult males often patrol alone, males are regularly attacked and killed by crowned eagles.<ref name= Mitani/> However, adult monkeys (52%) were found in almost equal measure as juvenile monkeys (at 48%) in Uganda.<ref name= Mitani/>

As various species and ages of primates are taken, primate prey varies in size. All diurnal African monkeys weigh in excess of {{cvt|2|kg|lb}} in adulthood.<ref name= Arlet>{{cite journal | last1=Arlet |first1=M. E. |last2=Isbell |first2=L. A. | year = 2009 | title = Variation in behavioral and hormonal responses of adult male gray-cheeked mangabeys (''Lophocebus albigena'') to crowned eagles (''Stephanoaetus coronatus'') in Kibale National Park, Uganda | journal = Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology | volume = 63 | issue = 4| pages = 491–499 | doi=10.1007/s00265-008-0682-5|bibcode=2009BEcoS..63..491A |s2cid=19923092 }}</ref> African primates weighing under {{cvt|2|kg|lb}}, are almost entirely arboreal and nocturnal.<ref name = Walker/> However, such primates, are actually allies of [[lemur]]s rather than [[monkey]]s, may occasionally also be hunted by crowned eagles. [[Potto]] (''Perodicticus potto'') are notable secondary prey in the Tai Forest and various [[galago]]s have been found across the range, such as in the [[Kiwengwa/Pongwe Forest Reserve]] where they made up 7.5% of the remains.<ref name="McGraw, W. S. 2006"/><ref name= Msuya/> Although no one has seen the eagles catch these primates, it is thought pottos and galagos are likely to be taken if discovered by an eagle while sleeping in dense [[Leaf|foliage]] during the day.<ref name= Mitani/> Among monkeys, Cercopithecine monkeys are on the small side. Full-grown female ''Cercopithecus'' can range in average weight from {{cvt|2.7|to|4.26|kg|lb}} and males from {{cvt|4.1|to|6.9|kg|lb}} depending on species.<ref name = Walker>{{cite book|author=Novak, R. M. |year=1999|title=Walker's Mammals of the World|edition=6th |publisher=[[Johns Hopkins University Press]] |location=Baltimore|isbn=0-8018-5789-9}}</ref><ref name= Struhsaker/><ref name="McGraw, W. S. 2006"/> Other major prey, such as [[vervet monkey]]s typically weighing {{cvt|5.5|kg|lb}} in males and {{cvt|4.1|kg|lb}} in females, but up to {{cvt|8|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite book|author=Skinner, J.D.|author2=Smithers, R.H.N. |year=1990 |title=The mammals of the southern African subregion |edition=2nd|location=Pretoria (South Africa) |publisher=University of Pretoria|page=771|isbn=0-86979-802-2}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | author = Cawthon Lang, K.A. | year = 2006 | url = http://pin.primate.wisc.edu/factsheets/entry/vervet | title = Primate Factsheets: Vervet (Chlorocebus) Taxonomy, Morphology, & Ecology | publisher = Primate Info Net | access-date = 2011-01-30}}</ref> [[Mangabey]]s and most [[colobus monkey]]s are larger, weighing considerably in excess of {{cvt|5|kg|lb}} at maturity.<ref name = Walker/> The adults of these species taken by crowned eagles usually weigh {{cvt|10.5|kg|lb}}in males and {{cvt|7|kg|lb}} in females,<ref name= Struhsaker/> but [[olive colobus]] are smaller, weighing less than {{cvt|5|kg|lb}},<ref name="McGraw, W. S. 2006"/> and large adults of [[western red colobus]] and [[Sooty mangabey]]s can weigh up to {{cvt|11|-|12|kg|lb}}.<ref name="Gilbert">{{Cite journal |last1=Gilbert |first1=Christopher C. |last2=McGraw |first2=W. Scott |last3=Delson |first3=Eric |date=July 2009 |title=Brief communication: Plio-Pleistocene eagle predation on fossil cercopithecids from the Humpata Plateau, southern Angola |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/ajpa.21004 |journal=American Journal of Physical Anthropology |language=en |volume=139 |issue=3 |pages=421–429 |doi=10.1002/ajpa.21004 |pmid=19241464 |bibcode=2009AJPA..139..421G |issn=0002-9483|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Larger [[king colobus]], [[mantled guereza]] and [[black-and-white colobus]] can weigh up to {{cvt|13.5|kg|lb}} in adult males.<ref>Ronald M. Nowak (1999). Walker's Primates of the World. JHU Press. pp. 38–39. ISBN 978-0-8018-6251-9.</ref><ref>Napier, P. H. (1985). Catalogue of Primates in the British Museum (Natural History) and Elsewhere in the British Isles, part III: Family Cercopithecidae, Subfamily Colobinae. London: British Museum (Natural History).</ref> Based on observation and literature, any monkey of a weight of up to {{cvt|10|to|15|kg|lb}} may reportedly be hunted.<ref>Daneel, A. (1979) ''Prey size and hunting methods of the crowned eagle''. Ostrich 50: 120–121.</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Cordeiro |first1=N. J. | year = 2008 | title = Behaviour of blue monkeys (Cercopithecus mitis) in the presence of crowned eagles (''Stephanoaetus coronatus'') | doi = 10.1159/000156660 | journal = Folia Primatologica | volume = 59 | issue = 4| pages = 203–206 }}</ref>

The crowned eagle's diet may extend to the young and juveniles of [[baboon]]s and similar species. [[yellow baboon]]s, [[olive baboon]]s (''Papio anubis''), [[chacma baboon]]s (''P. ursinus''), [[Drill (mammal)|drills]] (''Mandrillus leucophaeus'') and [[mandrill]]s (''M. sphinx''), all falling into the above maximum primate weight bracket and all have been successfully hunted.<ref name= Mitani/><ref name= Thomsett/><ref name="Cowlishaw, G. 1994">{{cite journal | last1 = Cowlishaw |first1=G. | year = 1994 | title = Vulnerability to predation in baboon populations | journal =  Behaviour| volume = 131 | issue = 3–4| pages = 293–304 | doi=10.1163/156853994x00488 |bibcode=1994Behav.131..293C }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Paciência, F. M. D.|author2= Baluya, D.|author3= Mbaryo, P.|author4= Knauf, S.|author5=Zinner, D. |year=2017|title=Olive baboons' (''Papio anubis'') response towards crowned eagles (''Stephanoaetus coronatus'') at Lake Manyara National Park|journal=Primate Biology |volume=4|issue= 1|pages=101–106|doi=10.5194/pb-4-101-2017|doi-access= free|pmid= 32110697|pmc= 7041538}}</ref> In [[South Africa]], remains of three adults or subadult [[chacma baboon]]s were found in the nest remain, suggesting adult female or subadult baboons can be possibly preyed upon.<ref name = 'Boshoff.1994'>{{cite journal|author=Boshoff, A. F.|author2=Palmer, N. G. |display-authors=1|title=Comparison of the diet of crowned eagles in the savanna and forest biomes of south-eastern South Africa|journal=South African Journal of Wildlife Research|year=1994|pages=26–31}}</ref> In many cases, baboons and drills may be actively avoided, as the adult males of these species, at up to twice as heavy as the female, are probably invulnerable to hunting and tend to be notoriously violent-tempered. In one case, olive baboons destroyed the nest of a crowned eagle pair after one of the eagles killed a baby in the baboon group.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://ngongforest.wildlifedirect.org/2012/01/20/the-african-crowned-eagle-makes-a-mistake-of-having-the-olive-baboon-for-dinner/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130309004936/http://ngongforest.wildlifedirect.org/2012/01/20/the-african-crowned-eagle-makes-a-mistake-of-having-the-olive-baboon-for-dinner/ |archive-date=2013-03-09 |title=The African Crowned Makes a Mistake of Having the Olive Baboon for Dinner |first=Steve |last=Kamotho |publisher=[[Wildlife Direct]] |access-date=2013-06-24}}</ref> When experimentally exposed to an image of a crowned eagle, a dominant male mandrill reacted aggressively towards it, implying that they will keep eagles at bay to protect the more vulnerable members of their troop.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.eve.ucdavis.edu/gpatricelli/Assets/Yorzinski&Vehrencamp,%20Antipred%20behavs%20in%20Mandrills,%20PrimateReports%202008.pdf |title=Mandrill antipredator behavior |publisher=[[University of California]] |access-date=2012-08-02 |vauthors=Yorzinski JL, Vehrencamp SL }}</ref> The crowned eagle is considered a potential predator of infant [[Common chimpanzee|chimpanzee]]s (''Pan troglodytes'') and [[bonobo]]s (''P. paniscus''), although this is unconfirmed.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Badrian |first1=Noel |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/978-1-4757-0082-4_11 |title=Feeding Ecology of Pan paniscus in the Lomako Forest, Zaire |last2=Malenky |first2=Richard K. |date=1984 |publisher=Springer US |isbn=978-1-4757-0084-8 |editor-last=Susman |editor-first=Randall L. |location=Boston, MA |pages=275–299 |language=en |doi=10.1007/978-1-4757-0082-4_11}}</ref> Although rarely observed, some monkeys will "bait" crowned eagles, which harass and provoke them. Leslie Brown described [[Sykes' monkey]]s (''Cercopithecus albogularis'') as rarely baiting eagles "with the insolent impunity of an expert [[Bullfighting|torero]] with a [[bull]]".<ref name= Leslie/>

===Ungulate===
[[File:Cape bushbuck (Tragelaphus sylvaticus) female.jpg|thumb|upright|A full-grown female [[Harnessed bushbuck|bushbuck]] (''Tragelaphus scriptus'') is the largest animal known to be hunted by crowned eagles.]]
Outside of the rainforest, the crowned eagle's diet tends to be somewhat more diverse. While they may take monkeys on a relatively small scale, other families, largely the small [[antelope]]s and [[Suidae|suid]]s (specifically, the [[red river hog]] (''Potamochoerus porcus'')), are the leading prey.<ref name= Leslie/><ref name=HBW/> In some wooded hillside nests in [[Kenya]], about half of the remains were from small antelope. The predominant prey species there is the [[Suni]] (''Neotragus moschatus'').<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Brown |first=Leslie H. |date=April 1971 |title=The Relations of the Crowned Eagle Stephanoaetvs Corona Tus and Some of ITS Prey Animals |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1971.tb05152.x |journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=113 |issue=2 |pages=240–243 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1971.tb05152.x |bibcode=1971Ibis..113..240B |issn=0019-1019|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In the Matumbi Hills of Tanzania, antelope make up about 30% of the dietary intake at nests, much of this being the Suni again.<ref name= Msuya/> Adults antelope of a similarly small size to the Suni, around {{cvt|5|kg|lb}} or slightly less, are readily hunted, such as [[Kirk's dik-dik]] (''Madoqua kirkii'') and the [[blue duiker]] (''Philantomba monticola'').<ref>WAINAINA, L. W. (2014). IMPACTS OF THE SOUTHERN BYPASS ROAD CONSTRUCTION THROUGH NGONG FOREST ON THE AFRICAN CROWNED EAGLE, NAIROBI COUNTY.</ref><ref>Jarvis, M. J. F., Currie, M. H., & Palmer, N. G. (1980). Food of crowned eagles in the Cape Province, South Africa. Ostrich, 51(4), 215-218.</ref> Larger antelopes are also taken, mainly calves but occasionally adults weighing around or more than {{cvt|10|kg|lb}} can be taken, including [[klipspringer]] (''Oreotragus oreotragus''), [[steenbok]] (''Raphicerus campestris''), [[Cape grysbok]] (''R. melanotis''), [[Sharpe's grysbok]] (''R. sharpei'') and about a half dozen small to medium sized [[duiker]]s, especially the [[red forest duiker]] (''Cephalophus natalensis'') and larger [[common duiker]] (''Sylvicapra grimmia'').<ref name= Msuya/><ref name = 'Boshoff.1994'/><ref>Swatridge, C. J., Monadjem, A., Steyn, D. J., Batchelor, G. R., & Hardy, I. C. (2014). Factors affecting diet, habitat selection and breeding success of the African Crowned Eagle Stephanoaetus coronatus in a fragmented landscape. Ostrich, 85(1), 47-55.</ref><ref name=PF>{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/eagles/crowned-hawk-eagle-0 | title=Crowned Hawk-eagle | the Peregrine Fund }}</ref> National Geographic recorded a video depicting a crowned eagle stalking a [[water chevrotain]] (''Hyemoschus aquaticus'') (the only [[Africa]]n representative of a small-bodied, [[deer]]-like family) along a rainforest river but show the chevrotain evading the eagle by submerging and swimming away from it.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=13GQbT2ljxs |title=Eagle vs. Water Chevrotain |date=26 July 2007 |via=[[YouTube]] |access-date=2013-06-24}}</ref> Even bigger [[Cape bushbuck|bushbuck]] and [[greater kudu]], mainly calves, are sometimes preyed upon regularly.<ref name=HBW/> Among accurately weighed bushbuck to be killed by a crowned eagle, some scaled {{cvt|15.9|kg|lb}}, {{cvt|20|kg|lb}}, or even {{cvt|28.8|kg|lb}} and {{cvt|30|kg|lb}} respectively in case of young ram, which about up to eight times the eagle's weight.<ref name= Leslie/><ref name= Ginn/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Malan |first1=Gerard |last2=Strydom |first2=Eleen |last3=Shultz |first3=Susanne |last4=Avery |first4=Graham |date=2016-05-20 |title=Diet of nesting African Crowned Eagles Stephanoaetus coronatus in emerging and forest–savanna habitats in KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.2989/00306525.2016.1183718 |journal=Ostrich |language=en |volume=87 |issue=2 |pages=145–153 |doi=10.2989/00306525.2016.1183718 |bibcode=2016Ostri..87..145M |issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>Grambo, Rebecca L. ''Eagles''. [[Voyageur Press]], 1999.</ref> Crowned eagles are known to hunt fawns of other largish antelopes including [[Thomson's gazelle]] (''Eudorcas thomsonii''), [[grey rhebok]] (''Pelea capreolus'') and [[impala]] (''Aepyceros melampus''), and trained eagles from re-introduction programme managed to kill adult female impalas and bushbucks.<ref name= Thomsett/><ref name= Batchelor>{{cite web |url=http://www.africanraptors.org/interview-with-garth-batchelor-about-the-african-crowned-eagle-in-south-africa/ |title=Interview with Garth Batchelor about the African Crowned Eagle in South Africa |first=Markus |last=Jais |date=21 April 2012 |publisher=African Raptors |access-date=2013-06-25}}</ref>

The taking of ungulates on a large scale, unlike primates, is not unique to the crowned eagle. The martial eagle was reported in [[Tsavo East National Park]] to hunt mainly [[dik-dik]]s and elsewhere has exceptionally killed large duiker weighing up to {{cvt|37|kg|lb}}.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref>{{cite journal|author=Smeenk, C. |year=1974|title=Comparative ecological studies of some East African birds of prey|journal=Ardea |volume=62|pages=1–97}}</ref> Equally or even more impressive feats of ungulate-hunting have been credited to the [[wedge-tailed eagle]] (''Aquila audax'') and especially the [[golden eagle]], despite these being considered less powerful than the crowned eagle.<ref name= Thomsett/> Both ''Aquila'' take mainly neonatal [[Sheep|lambs]] and [[deer]] fawns but can attack adult sheep and other large prey (i.e. full-grown [[kangaroo]]s, [[emu]]s, [[pronghorn]]s, [[reindeer]], etc.).<ref name= Watson>{{cite book|last=Watson |first=Jeff |title=The Golden Eagle|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aj5MNvCkun0C|year=2010|publisher=A&C Black|isbn=978-1-4081-1420-9}}</ref><ref name="Olsen">{{cite book |author=Olsen, P. |year=2005|title=Wedge-tailed Eagle|publisher=[[CSIRO Publishing]]|isbn=978-0-643-09314-0}}</ref> Even a [[bald eagle]] (''Haliaeetus leucocephalus''), usually target aquatic preys, was reportedly witnessed killing a pregnant adult sheep ewe.<ref>{{cite journal|author=McEneaney, T.|author2= Jenkins, M. |year=1983|title=Bald Eagle predation on Domestic Sheep|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=95|issue=4)|page=694}}</ref> The largest prey attacked by any living raptor is probably the up to {{cvt|114|kg|lb}} domestic [[Cattle|calves]] attacked and killed by golden eagles.<ref name= Phillips>{{cite journal | last1 = Phillips |first1=R. L. |name-list-style=amp |last2=Cummings |first2=J. L. |last3=Notah |first3=G. |last4=Mullis |first4=C. | year = 1996 | title = Golden eagle predation on domestic calves | journal = Wildlife Society Bulletin | volume = 24 | pages = 468–470 }}</ref>

===Other prey===
[[Hyrax]]es are known to be a major source of prey outsides of the rainforest.<ref name = 'Boshoff.1994'/><ref name=PF/> All six currently recognized species of hyrax have been hunted by crowned eagles. The greatest level of predation for this family has been directed at [[Southern tree hyrax]] (''Dendrohyrax arboreus''), but when they become locally abundant, [[rock hyrax]] (''Procavia capensis'') can become the species' preferred prey.<ref name= Leslie/> Other mammals have been recorded as opportunistic prey, including [[bat]]s, [[hare]]s (''Lepus'' sp.), [[Pedetes|springhares]] (''Pedetes'' sp.), [[cane rat]]s (''Thryonomys'' sp.), [[Cricetomys|giant pouched rats]] (''Cricetomys'' sp.), [[sun squirrel]]s (''Heliosciurus'' sp.), [[African giant squirrel]]s (''Protoxerus'' sp.), [[brush-tailed porcupine]]s (''Atherurus'' sp.), [[four-toed elephant shrew]]s (''Petrodromus tetradactylus'') and [[tree pangolin]]s (''Manis tricuspis'').<ref name= Mitani/><ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name= Thomsett/><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Mikula|first1=P.|last2=Morelli|first2=F.|last3=Lučan|first3=R. K.|last4=Jones|first4=D. N.|last5=Tryjanowski|first5=P.|year=2016|title=Bats as prey of diurnal birds: a global perspective|journal=Mammal Review|volume=46|issue=3|pages=160–174|doi=10.1111/mam.12060 |bibcode=2016MamRv..46..160M }}</ref><ref name=HBW/> Despite their obvious defenses and nocturnal habits, small [[Cape porcupine]]s (''Hystrix africaeaustralis'') have reportedly been taken in South Africa.<ref name= Batchelor/> These assorted mammals, generally smaller than primates and ungulates, are typically taken when preferred prey species are locally scarce.<ref name="Brown"/> Mammalian carnivores are sometimes also hunted ranging from smaller types such as [[yellow mongoose]] (''Cynictis penicillata''), [[banded mongoose]] (''Mungos mungo''), [[Crossarchus|cusimanses]], [[African palm civet]] (''Nandinia binotata'') or [[genet (animal)|genet]]s to larger varieties such as adult [[African wildcat]]s (''Felis lybica'') and [[black-backed jackal]]s (''Canis mesomelas'').<ref name= Thomsett/><ref name= Struhsaker/><ref name = 'Boshoff.1994'/><ref name= Msuya/>

Adult eagles will only resort to hunting large [[bird]]s when mammals are scarce, but in southern Africa, they can be a fairly common component of the diet. Bird prey can include [[guineafowl]], [[francolin]]s, [[ibis]], [[pigeon]]s, [[ostrich]] chicks (''Struthio camelus'') and the fledglings of [[heron]]s and [[stork]]s.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name= Batchelor/> One nest even had the remains of a [[marabou stork]] (''Leptoptilos crumeniferus''), which is a formidable species not usually susceptible to avian predators.<ref name= Thomsett/> [[Hornbill]]s may be the most widely represented bird in the diet and the [[black-casqued wattled hornbill]] (''Ceratogymna atrata'') reacts strongly to both the call of crowned eagles and the alarm calls of monkeys specified to the eagles (which are separately identifiable from the monkey's leopard-related alarm calls to both humans and, apparently, hornbills).<ref name= Mitani/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Slater |first1=Peter |last2=Rainey |first2=Hugo |last3=Zuberbühler |first3=Klaus |date=2004 |title=The responses of black-casqued hornbills to predator vocalisations and primate alarm calls |url=https://brill.com/view/journals/beh/141/10/article-p1263_5.xml |journal=Behaviour |volume=141 |issue=10 |pages=1263–1277 |doi=10.1163/1568539042729658 |bibcode=2004Behav.141.1263S |issn=0005-7959|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In [[Kenya]], [[snake]]s, including venomous varieties, may regularly supplement the diet.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> [[Monitor lizard]]s may also be hunted and, as in the martial eagle, the crowned eagle may attack even the largest African monitors, the adult [[Nile monitor]] (''Varanus niloticus'') and the [[Varanus albigularis|rock monitor]] (''V. albigularis'').<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kaneda |first=Hiroshi |date=2020-01-02 |title=Breeding behaviour and diet of the Crowned Eagle Stephanoaetus coronatus in western Tanzania |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.2989/00306525.2019.1701577 |journal=Ostrich |language=en |volume=91 |issue=1 |pages=57–63 |doi=10.2989/00306525.2019.1701577 |bibcode=2020Ostri..91...57K |issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name = 'Boshoff.1994'/><ref name= Msuya/>

Domestic animals, including [[chicken]] (''Gallus gallus domesticus''), [[turkey (bird)|turkey]]s (''Meleagris gallopavo''), [[cat]]s (''Felis catus''), small to medium-sized [[dog]]s (''Canis familiaris''), small [[pig]]s (''Sus domesticus''), [[Sheep|lambs]] (''Ovis aries''), and [[goat]]s (''Capra hircus''), are taken only when wild prey is greatly depleted.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name= Batchelor/> Juveniles and subadults, may take unconventional prey more frequently than adults.

===Interspecies conflicts and mortality===
In the rainforest interior, the crowned eagle occupies a unique niche and it is, by far, the largest and most dominant raptorial bird in such areas. Other large predators that may exploit similar prey in the same forested habitats include [[leopard]] (''Panthera pardus''), [[African golden cat]] (''Profelis aurata''), [[Nile crocodile]] (''Crocodylus niloticus''), [[dwarf crocodile]] (''Osteolaemus tetraspis''), [[Python sebae|African rock python]] (''Python sebae''), [[Common chimpanzee|chimpanzee]] and larger monkeys, like [[mandrill]] and [[baboon]]s.<ref name= Thomsett/><ref name= Leslie/><ref name= Baldellou/> All of these competitors are much heavier bodied than a crowned eagle, ranging in size from the {{cvt|10|kg|lb}} golden cat to the {{cvt|225|kg|lb}} Nile crocodile.<ref>''Carnivores of the World'' by Dr. Luke Hunter. [[Princeton University Press]] (2011), {{ISBN|9780691152288}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/reptiles/nile-crocodile.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070623181200/http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/reptiles/nile-crocodile.html |archive-date=23 June 2007 |title=Nile Crocodile |publisher=National Geographic |access-date=2011-04-03}}</ref> While the reptiles usually hunt on the ground or near water, [[Felidae|felids]], large monitor lizards and baboons can be assured thieves who will climb trees and take crowned eagle kills.<ref name= Leslie/> In a comparison of the monkey-based diets of rainforest crowned eagles with leopards and chimpanzees, the big cat was estimated to take prey averaging {{cvt|11.27|kg|lb}}, about twice the average estimated prey weight for crowned eagles in the same ecosystem, and the great ape {{cvt|6.9|kg|lb}}, about a kilogram more than that of the crowned eagle.<ref name= Shultz/> In South Africa, it is reported that [[Cape porcupine]]s and [[bushpig]]s (''Potamochoerus larvatus'') are attracted to trees used for prey consumption by crowned eagles, in order to scavenge the sinew and bone that's discarded to the ground.<ref name= Batchelor/> In more mixed eastern and southern habitats, the diversity of large predators is higher and the crowned eagle, despite its great power, is not assured at the top of the avian food chain. Amongst the more formidable raptors, the martial, the Verreaux's, and the crowned eagles, may live on the same hillside and all hunt [[hyrax]]es. While the Verreaux's eagle is something of a [[rock hyrax]] specialist, the martial eagle, even more so than the crowned eagle, has an extremely broad prey base. All these eagles will readily steal the prey caught by the other raptors.<ref>[http://www.africanraptors.org/interview-with-alan-kemp-about-the-martial-eagle-in-southern-africa/ Interview with Alan Kemp about the Martial Eagle in Southern Africa]. Africanraptors.org (29 August 2011). Retrieved on 2012-08-22.</ref> However, the great eagles are segregated by both their habitat preferences and main hunting techniques, which make it possible for the species to successfully nest within a few kilometers of one another. While the crowned eagle lives in denser woods and hunts from a perch, the martial eagle tends to live in more open wooded savanna habitats and tends to hunt on the wing at a high flying height (thanks to its superb vision) and the Verreaux's eagle lives in precipitous mountain habitats and tends to contour-hunt, hugging the uneven contours of the rocks while flying, only a few meters high. Like the martial eagle, the crowned eagle has been known to prey on smaller raptorial birds.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/>

Young and inexperienced crowned eagles may be killed as prey by large carnivores. Two eagles reintroduced into the wild were killed by predators, one by a leopard that surprised a male on a monkey kill in the rain, and the other by a crocodile that took a female as she ate a young bushbuck kill near the water's edge.<ref name= Thomsett/> In Kenya, cases of predation on nestlings and fledglings have reportedly involved [[honey badger]]s (''Mellivora capensis'') and [[cobra]]s.<ref name= Thomsett/> In one extraordinary case, an adult male [[Sanje mangabey]] (''Cercocebus sanjei''), estimated to weigh {{cvt|10|to|12|kg|lb}}, attacked an adult female crowned eagle that was trying to hunt his troop, jumping onto her back when she was in mid-flight and killing her with a strong bite.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jones |first1=T. |last2=Laurent |first2=S. |last3=Mselewa |first3=F. |last4=Mtui |first4=A. |name-list-style=amp |year=2004 |title=Sanje mangabey ''Cercocebus sanjei'' kills an African crowned eagle ''Stephanoaetus coronatus'' |journal=Folia Primatologica |volume=77 |issue=5 |pages=359–363 | doi = 10.1159/000093701|pmid=16912504 |s2cid=13093539 }}</ref> This is the first confirmed instance of a monkey killing an adult crowned eagle. In one case, a female who tried to hunt an adult female baboon was found seriously wounded after a male baboon interceded, though the eagle was captured, medically treated, and lived in captivity.<ref name= Thomsett/> In another case involving potential prey turning the tables, an eagle that was trying to hunt an incubating female [[Egyptian goose]] (''Alopochen aegyptiacus'') was quickly attacked by her mate, who flew at the eagle and bit repeatedly at it, causing the eagle to quickly withdraw.<ref name= Thomsett/> The average life expectancy for crowned eagles is 14 to 16 years, although some specimens live more than 30 years.<ref name="Brown"/><ref name= Thomsett/><ref>Brown, L. H. (1966). Observations on some Kenya eagles. Ibis, 108(4), 531-572.</ref>

==Relationship with humans==
[[File:Stephanoaetus coronatus.jpg|thumb|right|Early illustration of a young female crowned eagle]]
[[File:Neto Gamiz .jpg|thumb|In captivity, showing size]]

===Attacks on humans===
While several smaller raptorial birds will attack humans if they come too close to the nesting site, usually these have minor consequences for the human victim. Mother crowned eagles, in the post-fledging stage, readily attack any human who comes close to the nest. Adult males may also attack humans before fledging but only do so rarely.<ref name= Leslie/> Despite the size and power of the [[eagle]], attacks by the eagles may have minor consequences as well since the attacks are meant only to displace and not kill or seriously maim the intruding animal. However, nest-defense attacks may nonetheless possibly result in deep, painful, open wounds, which can lead to risk of infection or the need for stitches.

The crowned eagle is perhaps the only extant raptorial bird which has been believed to attack human children as prey, outside of few exceptional circumstances in the case of the [[Wedge-tailed eagle]] and the [[Martial eagle]].<ref name= Olsen/><ref>{{cite journal|author=Whitlock, F. L. |year=1928|title=Wedge-tailed Eagle Attacks Child|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=27|issue=4|pages=287–289|doi=10.1071/MU927286b |bibcode=1928EmuAO..27..287W |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/398433 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2016-07-14/eagle-attack-on-boy-photo-goes-viral/7630624|title=Photo of wedge-tailed eagle attacking boy goes viral|work=ABC Wimmera}}</ref> In one case, a 7-year-old boy, of a weight of approximately {{cvt|20|kg|lb}}, was ambushed by a crowned eagle, who gouged its talons through the boy's throat and chest. The attack was ended by a woman who came upon them and rescued the child by bludgeoning the eagle to death with a hoe.<ref name= Steyn/> In another case, the skull of a human child was found in the nest of a crowned eagle pair.<ref name= Steyn/> In yet another instance, when assisting in the investigation of the disappearance of a four-year-old girl, Simon Thomsett came to believe she was the victim of a crowned eagle after the severed arm of a child was found in a tall tree that was inaccessible to leopards and known to be used as a crowned eagle cache.<ref name= Thomsett/>

One other living eagle, the [[martial eagle]] (''Polemaetus bellicosus''), has been confirmed to take a human child in a possible predation attempt, a four-year-old boy in [[Ethiopia]]. Unlike the crowned eagle, the martial eagle is not a specialized primate hunter, and preys mainly on large ground-dwelling birds. The eagle in question attacked three children, killing one of them, before being shot by a villager.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Child killed in Ethiopia eagle attack |url=https://www.von.gov.ng/child-killed-in-ethiopia-eagle-attack |newspaper=[[Voice of Nigeria]]}} {{dead link|date=March 2022 |fix-attempted=yes}}</ref> In the famed [[Taung]] deposit in South Africa, a skull from a child ''[[Australopithecus africanus]]'', a possible ancestor of [[human being]]s, led to considerable speculation. Ultimately referred to as the [[Taung Child]] and estimated to weigh {{cvt|9|to|11|kg|lb}}, the child became the type specimen for its species. The child appeared to have died from a clean row of piercing to its skull. Scholarly examination of the piercings has led scientists to believe that the specimen was seemingly killed by an eagle, of which the crowned eagle is the most likely candidate.<ref name= Berger>{{cite journal | last1 = Berger |first1=L. R. |last2=Clarke |first2=R.
