J. | year = 1995 | title = Eagle involvement in accumulation of the Taung child fauna | journal = Journal of Human Evolution | volume = 29 | issue = 3| pages = 275–299 | doi=10.1006/jhev.1995.1060|bibcode=1995JHumE..29..275B }}</ref><ref name= Gilbert/> This predatory relationship has led to much hypothesizing as to whether ''Stephanoaetus'' eagles may have partially shaped human evolution, with small early primate ancestors having evolved towards larger body sizes and larger brains due to the reduced probability of eagle predation with these features.<ref name="McGraw, W. S. 2006"/><ref name= Thomsett/><ref name= Berger/>

===Conservation status===
[[File:Makere native with a great-crowned eagle, Herbert Lang.jpg|thumb|left|A [[Makere people|native Makere]] in the [[Democratic Republic of the Congo|DRC]] holds a dead adult crowned eagle.]] The crowned eagle is fairly common in suitable habitat, though at the population level, its numbers have shown a decline in sync with [[deforestation]]. Declines appear to be widespread and may be increasing due to the often fevered pace of clear-cutting.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> This species main habitat is rich, high-canopy forest, which is a major target of timber companies, agriculturists, palm oil and biofuel plantations and miners as well as slash and burn farmers. A [[charcoal]]-based economy outdoes [[mineral]]-based economics in [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]] and [[Zaire]], both fuel wars and makes inroads deep into previously virgin forests.<ref name= Thomsett/> As two of central Africa's largest businesses, this has a devastating effect on forests and wildlife. Charcoal taken from [[Kenya]] and [[Ethiopia]] is often thought to finance Somali warlords.<ref name= Thomsett/> [[Tanzania]], which is more heavily developed for agriculture (largely today for biofuel) than adjacent Kenya, has even more reduced forest habitat.<ref name= Thomsett/> The crowned eagle is far more common in protected areas and reserves than elsewhere in its range, though is still recorded consistently outside of these areas. Biologists in Africa now suspect that the crowned eagles adaptability to small, fragmented tracts of woodland has been exaggerated in the past.<ref name= Thomsett/> Some habitat losses have been offset by the establishment of exotic tree plantations, where this species can nest, but which generally lack a sufficient prey base.<ref name= Tarboton/> The crowned eagle in [[Ethiopia]] is certainly at very low density and restricted to protected areas. It may be obliged to utilize exotics stands, but it is unlikely to ever be capable of surviving in the complete absence of indigenous (and thus prey productive) forests.<ref name= Thomsett/> Certain southern African countries, such as [[Zambia]], [[Zimbabwe]] and [[Malawi]], have almost no extensive stands of native forest today, while other countries such as [[Namibia]] were never heavily forested.<ref name= Thomsett/> In 2012 the species status was changed to [[Near Threatened]] by the [[IUCN]].<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref>{{cite web|title=Recently recategorised species |url=http://www.birdlife.org/action/science/species/global_species_programme/whats_new.html |publisher=Birdlife International (2012) |access-date=15 June 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080914020126/http://www.birdlife.org/action/science/species/global_species_programme/whats_new.html |archive-date=14 September 2008 }}</ref> Like the [[martial eagle]], the crowned has throughout modern history been persecuted by farmers, who maintain that the bird is a threat to their livestock.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/> In fact, both the crowned and martial eagles only rarely attack livestock.<ref name=HBW/> In some cases, however, crowned eagles have actually been killed while attempting to hunt domestic animals.<ref name= Thomsett/> Another cause for persecution of the species is that crowned eagles are considered competitors in the illegal bushmeat and poaching trades. Within the forest land-locked countries of Africa, the [[bushmeat]] trade is the largest source of animal protein for humans.<ref name="Topp-Jorgensen">{{Cite journal |last1=Topp-Jørgensen |first1=Elmer |last2=Nielsen |first2=Martin Reinhardt |last3=Marshall |first3=Andrew R. |last4=Pedersen |first4=Ulrik |date=March 2009 |title=Relative Densities of Mammals in Response to Different Levels of Bushmeat Hunting in the Udzungwa Mountains, Tanzania |journal=Tropical Conservation Science |language=en |volume=2 |issue=1 |pages=70–87 |doi=10.1177/194008290900200108 |issn=1940-0829 |doi-access=free}}</ref> It is a multibillion-dollar business with some 5 million tons (mostly small antelopes and monkeys, the crowned eagle's staple diet) being killed each year.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Bowen-Jones |first1=E. |last2=Pendry |first2=S. | year = 1999 | title = The threat to primates and other mammals from the bushmeat trade in Africa, and how this threat could be diminished 1 | doi = 10.1017/s0030605300030581 | journal = Oryx | volume = 33 | issue = 3| pages = 233–246 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In just 500 million acres of the [[Congo Basin]] owned by 8 countries the weight equivalent to 40.7 million humans is removed each year (or 740,000 [[African forest elephant|bull elephants]]).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wilkie |first1=David S. |last2=Carpenter |first2=Julia F. |date=1999 |title=Bushmeat hunting in the Congo Basin: an assessment of impacts and options for mitigation |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1023/A:1008877309871 |journal=Biodiversity and Conservation |volume=8 |issue=7 |pages=927–955 |doi=10.1023/A:1008877309871|bibcode=1999BiCon...8..927W |url-access=subscription }}</ref> The effect of this unsustainable culling is to severely depress or remove the large, medium and small wildlife species of the forests. Crowned eagles require some {{cvt|430|kg|lb}} of "bush meat" a year and thus directly compete with the industry.<ref name= Thomsett/><ref name="Cowlishaw, G. 1994"/><ref name= Topp-Jorgensen/> The [[red colobus]], a monkey that typifies the optimal forest quality and is a main food species for crowned eagles, has been singled out as one of the fastest declining and most endangered monkeys in the world due primarily to the bushmeat trade.<ref>{{cite journal | last = Struhsaker |first=T. T. | year = 2005 | title = Conservation of red colobus and their habitats | journal = International Journal of Primatology | volume = 26 | issue = 3| pages = 525–538 | doi=10.1007/s10764-005-4364-0|bibcode=2005IJPri..26..525S |s2cid=20335688 }}</ref> In some cases, crowned eagles have reportedly even been shot by primate conservationists in a misguided attempt to mitigate their predation of declining primate species.<ref name= Thomsett/> It is estimated that 90% of the global distribution of the crowned eagle may be subject to habitual persecution or is even killed and eaten itself as bushmeat.<ref name= Thomsett/><ref name= Topp-Jorgensen/> On the other hand, some educated foresters and fruit-growers actually encourage protection of populations, due to the controlling effect crowned eagles have on populations of potentially harmful mammals.<ref name=RaptorsWorld/><ref name= Batchelor/> In April 1996 the world's first captive-born crowned eagle hatched at the [[San Diego Zoo]]. Among [[International Species Information System|ISIS]] registered zoos, only San Diego Zoo, [[San Francisco Zoo]], [[Los Angeles Zoo]], [[Fort Worth Zoo]] and [[Lowry Park Zoo]] house this species.<ref>ISIS (2010). ''[https://app.isis.org/abstracts/Abs53895.asp Stephanoaetus coronatus.]'' Version 27 October 2010</ref> Several wildlife rehabilitation centers in Africa house crowned eagles.<ref name= Thomsett/> Due to their high-strung dispositions, tendency for aggression towards humans and resistance to hunting prey via coercion and hunger, the crowned eagle is often considered to be poorly suited for [[falconry]]. However, there are several eagles of this species used as such in England and sometimes in Africa, where they have been reportedly used to cull locally overpopulated feral dogs.<ref name= Thomsett/>

非洲冠鵰，又稱王冠鵰、冕雕、冠雕、非洲王冠鵰、非洲冕雕、冕鷹鵰、冠鷹鵰、王冠鷹雕，是撒哈拉以南非洲最大的猛禽。在南部非洲主要棲息於河岸林地與各種森林中。非洲冠鵰是非洲冠鵰屬中的唯一物種，此屬中的另一物種馬達加斯加冠鵰於人們開拓馬達加斯加時滅絕。


參考
